findings.title: `ZStandardDecoder.decode` can silently drop data when a skippable frame is the *last* frame
findings.detail: The multi-frame loop condition is `while self.decompressor.eof and self.decompressor.unused_data`. If the zstd stream ends with a skippable frame that itself is followed by no further data, `unused_data` will be non-empty but after reassigning `self.decompressor` and calling `decompress(unused_data)` on it, the skippable frame may be consumed and the NEW decompressor will have `eof=False` (it saw a skippable frame, not a full zstd stream end). On the next iteration the loop exits, and `flush()` will then see `eof=False` and raise `DecodingError`. This exact edge case is partly masked by `# pragma: no cover` on the `flush` raise. The existing `test_zstd_multiframe` test only covers the case where a skippable frame appears *between* two regular frames. A test with a trailing skippable frame is missing and the behaviour is unspecified.

findings.title: `ZStandardDecoder.flush` incorrectly rejects valid streams that end with a skippable frame
findings.detail: `flush()` checks `if not self.decompressor.eof` and raises `DecodingError("Zstandard data is incomplete")`. After processing a multi-frame stream in `decode()`, `self.decompressor` points to the *last* freshly-created decompressor that consumed `unused_data`. If that unused_data was a skippable frame, its decompressor's `eof` will be `False` (a skippable frame is not a complete zstd stream), causing a spurious error. The `# pragma: no cover` comment hides this from the test suite.

findings.title: Truncated / placeholder error message in `ZStandardDecoder.__init__`
findings.detail: The `ImportError` message reads `"Using 'ZStandardDecoder', ..."` where `...` is a literal ellipsis. Compare with the analogous `BrotliDecoder` message which explains the problem clearly: `"Using 'BrotliDecoder', but neither of the 'brotlicffi' or 'brotli' packages have been installed."`. The zstd message appears to be an unfinished draft and will be confusing to users who haven't installed `zstandard`.

findings.title: `test_empty_content` and `test_decoders_empty_cases` parametrizations do not include `zstd`
findings.detail: The parametrized tests on lines 185 and 196 only include `b"deflate"`, `b"gzip"`, `b"br"`, and `b"identity"`. The new `zstd` decoder is not included, so empty-body behaviour and empty-decode behaviour for zstd are untested. `ZStandardDecoder.decode(b"")` and `ZStandardDecoder.flush()` on a never-decoded stream are not exercised.

findings.title: `zstd` missing from `__all__`
findings.detail: `__all__` at the bottom of `_compat.py` only lists `["brotli", "set_minimum_tls_version_1_2"]`. The newly added `zstd` symbol is imported by `_decoders.py` via `from ._compat import brotli, zstd`, so the omission from `__all__` doesn't break anything at runtime, but it is inconsistent with the pattern used for `brotli` and could confuse future readers/tools.

findings.title: `ZStandardDecoder.flush` returns `bytes(ret)` where `ret` is already `bytes`
findings.detail: `self.decompressor.flush()` returns `bytes` (it's a no-op, documented to return `b""`). Wrapping it again in `bytes(ret)` is redundant. Returning `ret` directly (or just `b""`) is cleaner and avoids a needless copy.

findings.title: `test_decoding_errors` parametrization does not include `zstd`
findings.detail: The parametrized decoding-error test only covers `deflate`, `gzip`, and `br`. Adding `b"zstd"` would verify that `DecodingError` is raised consistently from `ZStandardDecoder`, which is the pattern established by the existing `test_zstd_decoding_error` test but through a different code-path (the streaming one used by `test_decoding_errors`).

findings.title: Catching `AttributeError` and `ValueError` on `import zstandard` is over-broad
findings.detail: `except (AttributeError, ImportError, ValueError)` — catching `AttributeError` and `ValueError` during an import statement is unusual. A bare `import` can only raise `ImportError` (or a subclass such as `ModuleNotFoundError`). The extra exception types copied from urllib3's implementation may make sense there because they guard additional code, but here the try-block only contains `import zstandard as zstd`, so the extra catches are never triggered and only add noise. If the intent is defensive, a comment explaining *when* these could fire would help.

findings.title: `test_zstd_decoding_error` passes a `str` as `content`, not invalid zstd bytes
findings.detail: `compressed_body = "this_is_not_zstd_compressed_data"` is a plain `str`, not `bytes`. httpx's `Response(content=...)` accepts `str` as a content type? Checking `_content.py` would confirm, but if it does it may encode it as UTF-8 and that UTF-8 encoding might coincidentally fail zstd decompression for the wrong reason, or the test might be relying on an incidental codepath. The test should use `b"this_is_not_zstd_compressed_data"` (a bytes literal) to clearly express intent and avoid any accidental pass-through.

findings.title: CHANGELOG `## Added` section is missing `###` prefix
findings.detail: The existing CHANGELOG headings use three-hash `###` (e.g., `### Fixed`, `### Added`, `### Deprecated`), but the new entry uses two-hash `## Added`. This breaks the section hierarchy.

findings.title: Redundant `assert zstd is not None` in `decode`
findings.detail: `__init__` already raises `ImportError` if `zstd is None`, so by the time `decode` is ever called, `zstd` is guaranteed to be non-None. The assert is misleading because it implies `decode` could be reached with `zstd=None`. Remove it or replace with a comment.

findings.title: `ZStandardDecoder.decode` can silently drop data when a skippable frame is the *last* frame

The existing `test_zstd_multiframe` test only covers the case where a skippable frame appears *between* two regular frames. A test with a trailing skippable frame is missing and the behaviour is unspecified.
